SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper evaluates the approach across a broad set of 8 datasets covering multiple sequence-labeling tasks and compares to reasonable baselines and state-of-the-art models, which supports the main claims. Related work is reported as adequate and some limitations (small POS gains, training-efficiency concern) are discussed. However, hyperparameters are not reported and it is unclear from the summary whether thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses were performed on model components and the auxiliary objective, limiting full reproducibility and understanding of where gains arise. Overall the experimental evidence is solid with minor gaps in analysis and reproducibility.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses semi-supervised multitask learning for sequence labeling (a core NLP/ML topic) and integrates a language-modeling objective into bidirectional LSTM/CRF architectures, which is squarely within the scope of top venues. It evaluates on 8 diverse datasets (PTB, GENIA, CoNLL, CHEMDNER, JNLPBA, FCE, etc.) using standard metrics (entity-level F1, accuracy) and compares to strong baselines (Rei et al., Lample et al., Huang et al.), matching expectations for audience and evaluation standards. Code availability and adequate related-work positioning further indicate appropriate placement, with only minor concerns (hyperparameters not reported and an efficiency caveat) that do not undermine overall fit.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments across eight diverse datasets and comparisons to reasonable baselines (including SOTA models). No mathematical derivations are required for the proposed multi-task objective, and the paper acknowledges limitations (small POS gains, softmax efficiency). However, the experimental methodology has gaps: hyperparameters are not reported and there is no mention of statistical significance testing, which weakens reproducibility and confidence in the reported consistent improvements. Overall the technical approach and evidence are sound but minor methodological omissions justify a rating below perfect.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes adding a secondary language modeling objective to bidirectional LSTM sequence-labeling models (with optional CRF/character components) and shows consistent gains across 8 datasets without extra data—this is a clear, practically useful extension and is differentiated from prior work (baselines include Rei et al. 2016 and other state-of-the-art models). However, the idea is a relatively straightforward multi-task/semi-supervised augmentation of existing architectures rather than a fundamentally new method or problem formulation, so the novelty is moderate (incremental but non-trivial).

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (Rei et al. 2016, Huang et al. 2015, Lample et al. 2016, Collobert et al. 2011) across eight benchmark datasets using standard metrics (entity-level F1 and accuracy). It also acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably (noting POS-tagging baselines are close to an upper bound) and reports adequate related-work discussion. A notable omission is that hyperparameters are not reported, which may affect the fairness and reproducibility of the comparisons.

IMPACT.justification: The paper targets an important practical problem—improving sequence labeling—by proposing a lightweight semi‑supervised multitask LM objective that integrates with existing biLSTM/CRF/character architectures. The method yields consistent performance gains across eight diverse datasets (error detection, NER, chunking, POS) without requiring extra annotated or unannotated data and is applicable to state‑of‑the‑art frameworks, making it likely to be adopted and built upon. While not transformative (gains are small on POS where baselines are near upper bounds and there are noted efficiency concerns), it offers a broadly useful technique and opens modest new avenues for auxiliary LM objectives in sequence labeling.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear methodological progression (introducing the bidirectional LSTM + auxiliary language modeling objective and evaluating across multiple benchmarks), and the main contributions are stated early and unambiguously. The writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate, suggesting notation and exposition are consistent and easy to follow. Presentation weaknesses are minor but concrete: hyperparameters are not reported and implementation/training-efficiency details are lacking, which hinders reproducibility. Overall readable and clear, with only small presentation issues to address.

main_claims: Proposes a sequence labeling framework with a secondary language modeling training objective that learns to predict surrounding words for every word.

The proposed language modeling objective can be integrated with bidirectional LSTM sequence labeling architectures (and with CRF/character components) making it applicable to existing state-of-the-art sequence labeling frameworks.

The novel language modeling objective provided consistent performance improvements across multiple benchmarks (8 datasets covering error detection, NER, chunking and POS-tagging) without requiring any additional annotated or unannotated data.
_summary.methodology: Add a secondary language modeling objective to a bidirectional LSTM sequence labeling model (with optional CRF and character-level components), training the model to predict surrounding words for each token alongside the primary sequence labeling task.
datasets: CoNLL 2014 shared task (converted to error detection)
BioCreative IV Chemical and Drug corpus (CHEMDNER)
baselines: Rei et al. (2016) neural sequence labeling model (baseline architecture)
Collobert et al. (2011) and related neural sequence-labeling approaches
limitations: On POS-tagging datasets baseline performance is close to an upper bound, so the language modeling objective yields only small improvements.
Training efficiency could be improved (current implementation uses a basic softmax for language modeling; authors note noise-contrastive estimation or hierarchical softmax could be used).
